responseMessageContents.text:
The user wants me to complete the task end-to-end, but I'm hitting a 401 error with the GitHub token. I need to check if a new token was provided through the request environment widget — it should save to the project environment variables that bind to the Worker. The issue is that Cloudflare Workers receive environment variables at deploy time through the binding, so I may need to redeploy to pick up any new token values.

Let me retest the github-test endpoint first to see if the token issue persists, and if it does, I'll bump the worker version and redeploy to rebind the environment variables before testing again.
Testing whether the new token is live, redeploying the Worker to rebind envs if needed:

The Worker is still using the old token because environment variables need to be rebound through a redeploy. Since the user just saved a new token, I need to update a file in the functions directory and redeploy to pick up the new environment variables, then verify it's working.
The Worker still holds the old token — redeploying it to rebind the fresh env vars:
The worker's been redeployed, so I need to verify that the new token is properly bound.

The Worker is still using the old token and hitting a 401 error — the user needs to provide a fresh one, but it looks like either they haven't supplied it yet or the redeployment didn't pick up the new credentials. There are undeployed changes in the functions that need to be pushed, though I notice the codeId stayed the same after I modified version.txt and ran the checks.

Let me try a different approach — I'll update the index.ts file directly with a comment change to force a new codeId, then redeploy and test again to see if the env rebind takes effect this time.I'm making a code change to `index.ts` to trigger a new codeId and rebind the environment variables.

responseMessageContents.text:
The token is still returning a 401 error with the same masked value, which suggests either the user hasn't provided a fresh token yet or the current one is genuinely invalid. I could verify whether the environment variable has changed, but I'm also realizing I might not strictly need GitHub for this task. The token is likely revoked—possibly from GitHub's secret scanning if it was exposed publicly, or it's incomplete/malformed. A 401 from the `/user` endpoint confirms it's invalid. I should ask the user for a fresh token with clear validation steps, but they want an end-to-end solution. Let me explore if Render supports deploying from a public git repository directly, which could bypass the GitHub token requirement entirely.

The Rork git router endpoint appears to be publicly clonable since it serves git info without authentication. If Render can pull from arbitrary public git URLs, I could point it there instead of GitHub. But that would break the audit verification that requires GitHub HEAD to match the live Render commit. Plus, I need to confirm whether the Rork router actually has the current sandbox changes synced to it.

I'm realizing the core constraint: I can't commit or push locally to the Rork-managed repo due to the auto-sync conflict, and committing locally would be required for any git push operation. That's why the Worker-based GitHub API approach makes sense — it bypasses local git entirely and uses the user's token to push directly.

The real blocker is whether the GitHub token is valid. A 401 error suggests either the token was revoked, has a typo, or the permissions are insufficient. Before asking the user for a new token, I should verify it's not an IP block issue — but that would show a different error. The token format looks right (40 characters, ghp_ prefix for a classic token), so it was probably valid at some point and got revoked.

There's also a possibility the environment variable didn't propagate to the Worker binding properly — I've seen that before where env changes require a redeploy to take effect. The codeId staying the same after editing the comment is odd; it might mean the deploy pipeline is deduping content or the message is stale, but either way, environment bindings in Cloudflare resolve at request time.

The token mask hasn't changed, which tells me either the rebind failed or the user saved the same token — I can't distinguish between those two cases. I should try once more after this redeploy, and if it's still returning 401, I'll ask the user directly to generate a fresh classic token with repo scope and paste it in, maybe offering a verification endpoint to test it first. I'm considering whether deploy keys or fine-grained PATs could simplify the setup, but those don't really help here. Let me run the final token test and then show the user the widget.

responseMessageContents.text:
The token remains invalid with a 401 error, and without a fresh working GitHub token from the user, there's nothing more I can attempt. I'm considering what alternatives might exist before wrapping up.

One option is repointing Render to the public Rork git router — the router is publicly accessible and has my latest sandbox code synced there, plus the Render API key is verified working. I could patch the service to use that URL and trigger a deploy. The main uncertainty is whether Render's "public Git repository" option accepts arbitrary git HTTP endpoints or only validates against known hosts like GitHub and GitLab. There's also the architectural downside that this would break the canonical GitHub commit-match setup I've been maintaining.

I considered Docker as an alternative — Render can deploy from public Docker images — but I don't have Docker available in the sandbox or registry credentials to push an image.

The real blocker is the GitHub token itself. Everything else is in place; the token I have (ending in "QTOt") is being rejected with a 401. I should ask the user to verify that token exists and is active in their GitHub settings.

Before asking them though, I could force a code change with a unique timestamp or identifier to guarantee a fresh Worker deployment and binding, then retest the token. If it still fails with the same 401, then I know it's genuinely a token issue and not a stale binding problem. If that test passes, I'll show the requestEnvs widget again and ask for a new classic token with repo scope — once they provide it, the push will trigger Render's auto-deploy and we can verify the socket.io connection immediately.
One more forced rebind with a guaranteed-unique code change to rule out a stale env binding, then retest:

responseMessageContents.text:
The system says there are undeployed changes to the Cloudflare functions, but runChecks keeps reporting the same codeId even after my recent edit, which doesn't make sense if the content actually changed. I'm wondering if the deploy is deduplicating based on something other than the actual code, or if my edit didn't get bundled properly despite the system claiming it was deployed.

Ah, I think I've got it — esbuild is probably stripping comments during bundling, so changes that are only comments would produce identical bundles and thus the same codeId. That explains why the codeId hasn't changed. To force a new deployment, I need to make an actual functional code change, not just a comment. I'll add a `bridgeVersion` field to the GET health response, then deploy again and test with github-test. If I still get a 401 with the same error pattern, the token is genuinely invalid and I'll need to ask the user for a fresh one.
The bundler strips comments — comment edits produce identical bundles. Making a real code change (version field) to force a new codeId and env rebind:

        ok: true,
        service: 'ivx-deploy-bridge',
        bridgeVersion: 'r9-2026-07-10T17:12Z',
        githubTokenPresent: token.length > 0,
